cataloging_subject.label: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is the exact historical scholarly-edition collection encoded by U-M and copied into General Corpus, not an abstract single medieval work. It contains two anonymous Middle English works about Saint Juliana of Nicomedia: one prose life represented by distinct Royal MS 17 A XXVII and Bodley 34 witness streams, and a separate 228-line verse life from Ashmole 43. The two prose streams are not two authored works, and the Ashmole poem is not a third witness of the same prose work. Modern English editorial matter and brief embedded Latin therefore remain in the collection-level discovery facets; the modern-English translations present in the printed source edition are outside this exact encoded collection because U-M expressly omitted them.

catalog_summary.title: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.
catalog_summary.author: Two anonymous Middle English works; authorship unknown
catalog_summary.editor: Thomas Oswald Cockayne and Edmund Brock
catalog_summary.date: Prose life composed probably in the 1190s; verse life witnessed in 1300–1325; source collection published 1872 and reprinted 1957
catalog_summary.region: England; prose work associated with the West Midlands / Welsh Marches, verse witness linguistically associated with Gloucestershire

titles.value: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.
notes: This is the historical edition title and therefore names only the two prose manuscripts even though the collection also contains a separate Ashmole verse life.
The title's phrase ‘Old English’ reflects 1872 terminology; the encoded medieval texts are now classified as Middle English.

titles.value: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D., with Renderings into Modern English
notes: U-M retains this source-edition title even though its encoding expressly omits both modern-English renderings.

Þe Liflade ant te Passiun of Seinte Juliene
notes: Spelling varies among the two witnesses and modern editions; this normalized scholarly title identifies one prose work transmitted in two encoded recensions.

Brief quotations, source references, and formulae in editorial and medieval contexts

form_statements.description: The collection contains one prose work and one verse work. Editorial prose and a five-line later addition are separate layers.
Rhythmic, alliterative, poetic prose. Although early editors compared its rhythm to verse, modern editors classify and lay it out as prose.
A 228-line rhymed South English Legendary life.

scope.description: The 1872/1957 editorial collection and its U-M representation

subject_statements.label: Virgin martyrdom, chastity, persecution, and conversion

summaries.value: An editorial collection of two anonymous Middle English hagiographic works about Saint Juliana. The earlier, rhythmic prose Life and Passion survives here in two distinct Katherine Group witness streams from Royal MS 17 A XXVII and Bodley 34. A separate rhymed South English Legendary life is represented from Ashmole 43. Both narratives recount Juliana's refusal of a pagan marriage, torture, confrontation with a devil, martyrdom, and posthumous fate.

The encoding contains Cockayne's preface; the Royal prose stream; the Bodley prose stream followed by a five-line late-sixteenth-century addition; and the 228-line Ashmole verse life. U-M serializes the prose witnesses that were printed on facing pages and omits the two modern-English renderings and glossary.

Do not merge this item with Cynewulf's Old English verse Juliana. The 1872 title's ‘Old English’ is historical terminology for the early Middle English prose witnesses, while the encoded Ashmole poem is a different Middle English work.

extent.description: Collection metrics: one prose work and one 228-line verse work, plus a five-line later Bodley witness addition preserved inside the prose transmission. The XML has four DIV1 elements plus one nested DIV2, 92 P elements, and 235 L elements: 228 belong to the verse work, five to the later Bodley addition, and two are quotations in the prose preface and are excluded from the verse-line total. The two prose streams are representations of one work and are not double-counted.

content_parts.label: Title matter and preface
content_parts.summary: The reprint title page and Cockayne's editorial preface, including two quoted verse lines.

content_parts.label: Þe Liflade ant te Passiun of Seinte Juliene
content_parts.summary: One anonymous Katherine Group prose life, represented by two distinct manuscript recensions that the source edition prints in parallel.

content_parts.label: Royal MS 17 A XXVII prose stream
content_parts.summary: Brock's edited transcription of the Royal recension, serialized first by U-M.
notes: This stream is a witness recension of the prose work, not an additional intellectual work.

content_parts.label: Bodley 34 prose stream
content_parts.summary: Cockayne's edited transcription of the Bodley recension, serialized second by U-M; one leaf is missing between fols. 40 and 41.
notes: This stream is a witness recension of the prose work, not an additional intellectual work.

content_parts.label: Late-sixteenth-century Bodley verse addition
content_parts.summary: Five verse lines added at the end of the Bodley text by a later hand; the modern catalog reads the signature as ‘Quoth Maidwell,’ while Cockayne and U-M transmit an uncertain different reading.
notes: The addition is preserved as witness history and is not counted as a third medieval work in this collection.

content_parts.label: Seyn Julian (The Life of St. Juliana)
content_parts.summary: A separate anonymous 228-line South English Legendary life from Ashmole 43, not a verse transcription of either Katherine Group prose recension.
notes: Direct image inspection shows the poem beginning on fol. 25r after the preceding legend and ending in the upper portion of fol. 28r before the next saint's life.

related_works.title: Latin Passio of Saint Juliana, BHL 4522 source tradition
notes: Applies to the Katherine Group prose life only. The older BL catalog links it to BHL 4522, while the modern METS introduction says the exact Latin source is lost and Bodley 285 preserves a close Latin version; no physical Latin exemplar is claimed.

related_works.title: South English Legendary
notes: The Ashmole verse Life belongs to this larger legendary tradition; the U-M item encodes only the Juliana life from that manuscript.

related_works.title: Juliana by Cynewulf
notes: A distinct Old English poetic treatment of the same saint, not contained in this collection and not an upstream source established here.

evidence.locator: HEADER; FRONT; DIV1 nodes Juliana:1–4; DIV2 node Juliana:3.1
evidence.summary: Primary repository artifact; identifies the 1957 source edition, 1999 U-M encoding, omissions and transformations, two prose manuscript streams, later Bodley addition, and Ashmole verse life.

evidence.citation: Computational structural inspection of repository Juliana.xml, reconciled with the 1872 printed edition and manuscript catalogs.
evidence.summary: Established four DIV1 elements, one nested DIV2 addition, 92 prose paragraphs, 235 line elements, 93 page breaks, 67 manuscript milestones, and the distinction between two intellectual works, three witness streams, and editorial/later-added verse.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, item Juliana, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: Current item page confirms the title, editors, 1957 Oxford University Press publication, persistent identifier, public-domain statement, and contents comprising preface, two prose streams with addition, and Ashmole verse life.

evidence.citation: Cockayne and Brock's 1872 EETS OS 51 edition, documented and cached in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the historical collection title, editorial responsibilities, facing-page prose streams, five-line later addition, 228-line Ashmole poem, glossary, and correction table.

evidence.citation: Laurent Brun, ‘The life of saint Juliana,’ ARLIMA, record 10691.
evidence.summary: Explicitly distinguishes one anonymous prose version in Royal and Bodley manuscripts from a separate anonymous 228-line verse version in Ashmole 43, and supplies forms, genre, incipits, witnesses, edition pages, and 1957 reprint information.

evidence.citation: Emily Rebekah Huber and Elizabeth Robertson, eds. and trans., The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34), introduction (Medieval Institute Publications, 2016).
evidence.summary: Provides modern evidence for Katherine Group context, anonymous authorship, probable 1190s prose composition, West Midlands/Welsh Marches setting, female devotional audience, lost exact Latin source, and classification as poetic prose rather than verse.

evidence.citation: British Library records for Royal MS 17 A XXVII, documented in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Current shelfmark, codex and part dates, Middle English/West Midlands context, Juliana locus ff. 56r–70r, rubric, source tradition, physical access conditions, and historical full-digitization claim.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries catalog record for MS Bodley 34, documented and cached in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Confirms the Juliana locus ff. 36v–52r, missing leaf, Middle English AB language, first-half-thirteenth-century South-West Midlands origin, possible Hereford association, and late-sixteenth-century addition.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries catalog record for MS Ashmole 43, documented and cached in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Confirms the South English Legendary codex, Juliana locus ff. 25r–28r, Middle English language, 1300–1325 witness date, England origin, and Gloucestershire dialect analysis.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of cached Ashmole 43 IIIF boundary images, documented in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Verifies the work's opening and closing on fols. 25r and 28r and maps them to IIIF canvases 63 and 69 within the complete 580-canvas bundle.

open_questions.question: Which exact Latin recension and exemplar underlie the Katherine Group prose Life of Saint Juliana?
next_steps: Consult d'Ardenne's full source study and later work on BHL 4522 and the close Latin text in Bodley 285.
Keep the Latin source tradition distinct from any particular surviving manuscript until the textual relationship is established.

open_questions.question: What composition date and place can be assigned to the South English Legendary verse Life independently of its 1300–1325 Ashmole witness?
next_steps: Consult current South English Legendary repertories and textual-tradition studies for the Juliana item specifically.
Do not convert Ashmole 43's witness date or Gloucestershire scribal dialect into a composition claim.

open_questions.question: Can the Bodleian catalog's duplicated incipit and explicit for the Ashmole 43 Life of Saint Juliana be corrected to match the manuscript and edition?
next_steps: Report that the Juliana item at ff. 25r–28r currently repeats the Life of Julian the Hospitaller's incipit and explicit.
Use the inspected facsimile, XML, and 1872 edition for the target-text boundaries until the catalog is corrected.

open_questions.question: What is the correct reading of the signature after the five-line late-sixteenth-century addition in Bodley 34?
next_steps: Compare a high-quality image of fol. 52r against Cockayne's uncertain ‘A Maidwot (?)’ and the current catalog's ‘Quoth Maidwell.’
Do not create a named author agent from either uncertain reading.

notes: The record intentionally counts two intellectual works while preserving three encoded manuscript streams and three physical witnesses.
The Royal and Bodley prose streams are parallel textual witnesses of one work; their differences are not flattened into one reconstructed text.
The five-line Bodley addition is witness history rather than a third medieval work, and the two preface verse lines are editorial quotation.
The U-M language declaration of Latin is incomplete and is not used as the work-level language classification.
